metadata.title: Claude, On Call
metadata.subtitle: The Support Plugin
metadata.audience: Practitioners and makers following the channel's Claude workflows
metadata.one_idea: If you have customers you have support; the support plugin drafts consistent, on-brand responses and turns recurring questions into reusable answers — a force multiplier for a small team, with the human staying on the judgment calls and the angry-customer edge cases.

props.command: I run support solo. Can Claude actually lighten the load, or just add a queue to double-check?
output: and turns repeat questions into answers you write once.

beats.narration_text: Jambo — this is Liam, in for Bear. If you have customers, you have support — and it doesn't wait. Install the support plugin and Claude triages the queue, drafts on-brand replies, and turns the questions you keep answering into answers you write once. Here's how it works, and where you stay on the line.

props.command: I run support solo. Can Claude actually lighten the load, or just add a queue to double-check?
output: and turns repeat questions into answers you write once.

beats.narration_text: Start with the problem everyone with customers already has.

props.title: The Reactive Burden
props.sub: Start with the problem everyone with customers already has.

beats.narration_text: One truth first: if you have customers, you have support. It arrives whether you're ready or not — a queue that fills overnight, questions that don't wait until morning. Left alone, it eats your day in fragments.
beats.show: A still of an inbox glowing in a dark room after hours; slow push toward the unread queue.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a lit screen or inbox in a dark room, after-hours, documentary duotone. No real UI, no logos.

beats.narration_text: The support plugin turns that burden into a process. For a solo operator, it's the difference between reacting all day and running a system that handles the queue for you.
beats.show: A chaotic scatter of incoming tickets resolves into a single ordered pipeline that flows off-screen.

beats.narration_text: For a small team, it does something else: it makes one consistent voice. Instead of five people answering five different ways, every reply carries the same tone and the same facts.
beats.show: Five differently-styled reply bubbles converge and snap into a single unified voice.

beats.narration_text: Under the hood, it does four concrete jobs.

props.title: What It Does
props.sub: Under the hood, it does four concrete jobs.

beats.narration_text: Triage the queue. Draft the replies. Build a knowledge base. And read sentiment — who's calm, and who's about to walk. Not magic; four jobs it does well.
beats.show: Four labeled chips resolve onto the stage: Triage · Draft · Knowledge base · Sentiment.

beats.narration_text: Triage first. As requests land, it sorts them: what needs you now, what can wait, and what's a known issue with a standard answer. The queue stops being a wall and becomes a sorted list.
beats.show: An incoming pile fans into three labeled lanes — Now · Later · Known — each lit in turn.

props.label: Sort the wall.
props.caption: Triage first. As requests land, it sorts them: what needs you now, what can wait, and what's a known issue with a standa

beats.narration_text: Then drafting. It generates replies that are helpful, on-brand, and fit the moment — pulling from your configured tone, your knowledge base, and the specific request in front of it. A first draft, not a form letter.
beats.show: Three input streams — tone, knowledge base, this request — converge into one drafted reply.

beats.narration_text: Third, sentiment. Not every request is equal — it flags the frustrated and the at-risk, the customer who needs a human now. It points your attention at the messages that actually need it.
beats.show: A grid of neutral message cards; one is ringed and lifted in terracotta — the at-risk customer.

props.label: Find the ones who matter.
props.caption: Third, sentiment. Not every request is equal — it flags the frustrated and the at-risk, the customer who needs a human n

beats.narration_text: And fourth, it builds a knowledge base. Every question you've ever answered is raw material; the plugin finds the common ones and drafts a structured FAQ you can publish. Your scattered experience becomes a help center.
beats.show: A still of loose notes and message scraps gathered into one bound reference; slow push-in.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: scattered sticky-notes / paper scraps gathering toward a bound manual or FAQ binder. No real UI.

beats.narration_text: For richer help, it connects to the support tools you already use — reading from your existing inbox instead of a blank slate. It works on your real conversations, not hypothetical ones.
beats.show: A connective line runs from the plugin into an existing support inbox; tickets flow in. No named product logos.

props.note: generic support-inbox glyph only; no vendor names on screen

props.label: Reach into your inbox.
props.caption: For richer help, it connects to the support tools you already use — reading from your existing inbox instead of a blank

beats.narration_text: One setup step makes it yours. You configure the tone — warm, efficient, or both — and you set escalation triggers: the words and situations that skip the standard answer and route straight to you. You decide what always reaches a human.
beats.show: A tone dial settles between 'warm' and 'efficient'; a row of trigger keywords reroutes a ticket toward 'you'.
beats.spark_line: Set the tone, set the tripwires.

props.label: Set the tone, set the tripwires.
props.caption: One setup step makes it yours. You configure the tone — warm, efficient, or both — and you set escalation triggers: the

beats.narration_text: Here's what an ordinary day actually looks like.

props.title: A Day on Support
props.sub: Here's what an ordinary day actually looks like.

beats.narration_text: Morning. You hand it the overnight queue and ask the three questions that matter: what's urgent, what's routine, and what can I answer in a line? Claude categorizes, drafts the easy ones, and flags the rest for you.
beats.show: Onda code-block: the composer typing the morning-triage prompt.

props.code:
Here are the support messages from the last 12 hours.
What's urgent? What's routine?
What can I answer with a quick reply?

props.title: Cowork
props.code:
Here are the support messages from the last 12 hours.
What's urgent? What's routine?
What can I answer with a quick reply?

beats.narration_text: The payoff is time. Your first thirty focused minutes handle what a morning of context-switching might otherwise stretch into two lost hours.
beats.show: A long fragmented bar labeled 'two hours' collapses into one tight block labeled 'thirty minutes'.

beats.narration_text: Now a hard one. A customer's payment has failed three times. They've been with you two years, and they're angry. You ask for a reply that's empathetic, addresses the failure, and offers a concrete fix.
beats.show: A still — a person at a screen showing a declined-payment notice; slow push toward the detail.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a frustrated person at a laptop with a generic 'declined' notice on screen; documentary duotone. No real brand UI. Start of vox_run R1.

beats.narration_text: Claude drafts in your tone — it acknowledges the frustration, explains the issue, and proposes a path forward. You read it, add a personal line, and send. The plugin caught the tone; you caught the context.
beats.show: Continuous pull-back from the same scene to the composed reply resting on the desk; same room grammar as B13.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: same duotone treatment; a composed reply / letter on the same desk. Continuity with B13 (vox_run R1, final beat).

beats.narration_text: Third moment: building the docs. Based on the requests you've handled this quarter, what are the ten questions that come up most? Claude finds the patterns and drafts the answers; you check them and publish.
beats.show: A stream of past tickets accumulates; the repeated questions rise and rank to the top ten.

beats.narration_text: And every entry pays forward. Each FAQ answer you publish is one question you never field again — support volume that quietly falls instead of climbing.
beats.show: Support history flows in on the left; published FAQ entries on the right divert future tickets away.

viz.target: published FAQ that deflects future tickets

props.destApp: published FAQ that deflects future tickets

beats.narration_text: Three rules keep all of this honest.

props.title: You Stay on the Line
props.sub: Three rules keep all of this honest.

beats.narration_text: Draft, don't autosend. Build the knowledge base a little at a time. And read the patterns — because what customers keep struggling with is product feedback in disguise.
beats.show: Three caution chips resolve: Draft, don't autosend · Build progressively · Patterns are feedback.

beats.narration_text: Draft, don't autosend, is the load-bearing one. Claude catches the right tone and content; your judgment catches the context it can't see — the angry customer, the exception, the account you know is fragile. Review before it goes.
beats.show: A still of a person reading a drafted reply, hand paused near send; a terracotta underline draws on beneath 'review'.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a person reviewing a message on screen, hand hovering near send; underline annotation added at previz. No real UI.

beats.narration_text: And watch the patterns. The plugin shows you what customers repeatedly can't figure out — and each recurring question is a signal about what to fix upstream, so the request stops coming at all. Support becomes a feed into the product.
beats.show: Recurring support requests cluster, then one arrow turns back toward 'the product' and the cluster thins.

viz.to: a product fix that removes them

beats.narration_text: The plugin drafts. You decide what sends. That's the whole contract.

Let's recap with Claude. If you have customers, you have support — and the plugin turns it from a reactive burden into a process. It does four jobs: triage the queue, draft on-brand replies, build a knowledge base, and read sentiment. It makes recurring questions into reusable answers, so volume falls instead of climbing. And it drafts — you decide: you review before anything sends, and you keep the angry-customer edge cases and the judgment calls.

props.command: I run support for a small business and want to set up the support plugin. First, ask me three things: the five questions my customers ask most, the tone my replies should carry, and the situations that must always reach me personally. Then draft FAQ answers for those five, a reusable response template in my tone, and a short list of escalation triggers I should configure.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude: I run support for a small business and want to set up the support plugin. Have it ask you three things first — the five questions your customers ask most, the tone your replies should carry, and the situations that must always reach you personally. Then it drafts FAQ answers for those five, a reusable template in your voice, and the escalation triggers to configure. It turns this chapter into your actual support setup — run it and watch what it asks. Liam, in for Bear.
